picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Alphabet delivered Q1 2026 EPS of $5.11, up 82% YoY, with Google Cloud growing 63% to a record $460B backlog — the fastest cloud growth in unit history. Berkshire tripled its stake to ~57.8M shares in Q1 2026, making GOOGL its 5th-largest holding, while the stock trades at a discount to pure-play tech on an earnings-growth-adjusted basis. At ~$388, the stock is 5% below its 52-week high of $408.61, offering a constructive entry near resistance that has become support after the Q1 earnings breakout.
picks.catalyst: Google I/O developer conference AI agent announcements and next-gen Gemini rollout accelerating Cloud enterprise deals
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$388 vs. 52-week range of $162–$408; forward P/E of ~22x on 25%+ EPS growth is materially cheaper than NVDA/META on a PEG basis; consolidating just below all-time high
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 40% YoY, search ad revenue share materially lost to AI competitors, or DOJ antitrust breakup order materially adverse
picks.keyRisk: Antitrust regulatory action forcing structural separation of Search and/or Android ecosystem

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom reported 106% AI revenue growth and 24% total revenue growth, with analysts projecting AI semiconductor revenue reaching $100B run rate by 2027. Druckenmiller opened a new AVGO position in Q1 2026 while institutional accumulation remains strong — D1 Capital added 622K shares. Custom ASIC and networking silicon provides a differentiated, durable moat distinct from NVDA's GPU dominance, satisfying the correlation-diversity requirement across two different AI value-chain nodes.
picks.catalyst: Fiscal Q2 2026 earnings report (expected late May/June) with AI revenue guidance raise and hyperscaler custom ASIC design win announcements
picks.entryNote: Up ~19% YTD; Evercore ISI raised PT to $582 from $490; trading at a meaningful discount to NVDA on forward P/E basis given custom silicon's more predictable revenue profile; Strong Buy consensus from 26 buys
picks.exitTrigger: Loss of major hyperscaler custom ASIC design win (Google TPU, Microsoft Maia in-sourcing acceleration) or AI capex pause materially reducing custom chip orders
picks.keyRisk: Hyperscaler insourcing of custom silicon accelerates, displacing Broadcom's co-design revenue

picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA posted Q1 FY2027 revenue of $82B (+85% YoY), EPS of $1.87 beat consensus of $1.77, record $49B FCF, and 74.9% gross margins — with next earnings on August 26. The average 12-month price target of $269 implies ~25% upside from current ~$214 levels, and BofA reiterated NVDA as a top AI spending pick on May 26. Despite the 'sell the news' reaction post-earnings, supply still cannot keep up with demand for Blackwell, making this a fundamentals-supported position rather than a pure momentum trade.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings August 26 with Blackwell GB200 ramp and Rubin architecture roadmap — the next proof point of whether the $82B quarterly revenue base can scale
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$214, off ATH of $236.54; ~40x forward EPS on 46% projected 3-5yr EPS CAGR is defensible; consolidating at EMA9/20 cluster — a technically constructive reset from parabolic extension
picks.exitTrigger: Gross margins compress below 70%, confirmed AMD MI400 share gains at hyperscaler tier, or new China export control restrictions materially reducing addressable market
picks.keyRisk: U.S.-China trade escalation reintroducing chip export controls, directly removing NVDA's China data center revenue

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: AWS revenue accelerated to +28% YoY growth in Q1 2026 — its biggest Q4-to-Q1 sequential increase ever — with operating margins expanding quarter over quarter. Tepper nearly doubled his AMZN stake (+98%), Ackman added ~1.84M shares to make it his 2nd-largest position, and Berkshire's exit was a manager-departure artifact (Combs leaving for JPMorgan), not a thesis change. At ~$215–220, the stock continues to build on its YTD outperformance of SPY with Jefferies adding it to Franchise Picks in mid-May.
picks.catalyst: AWS re:Invent 2026 (typically November/December) product roadmap and Q2 2026 earnings showing continued AWS acceleration above 28%
picks.entryNote: Strong YTD momentum; AWS operating margin expansion story still in early innings; retail AI and advertising flywheel adding incremental FCF; AMZN remains cheaper than MSFT on forward EV/FCF basis
picks.exitTrigger: AWS growth decelerates below 20%, cloud infrastructure capex ROI deteriorates materially, or retail segment margin compression from energy/fuel cost pass-through
picks.keyRisk: Massive 2026 AI capex ($75B+) compresses near-term FCF yield, triggering a multiple compression in a higher-for-longer rate environment

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman reported 14% revenue growth and 19% net income growth in Q1 2026, with equity trading revenue up 27% and investment banking recovering sharply. CFRA has a Buy rating with a $1,000 price target against a ~$988 close on May 21. In a late-cycle regime with elevated nominal GDP, M&A activity recovering, and capital markets re-accelerating, GS is the highest-quality pure-play on the investment banking/capital markets cycle — ROIC and ROE are expanding, not deteriorating.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (July) showing continued investment banking fee acceleration and capital markets volume uplift from post-Iran-conflict M&A normalization
picks.entryNote: Trading near CFRA's $1,000 PT; forward P/E ~12x vs. 5-year avg of ~11x — modest premium for structurally higher ROE; financial sector saw the largest positive revenue surprises in Q1 and earnings revisions trending up
picks.exitTrigger: Investment banking revenues reverse sharply, credit spreads widen materially (HY OAS >200bp), or a market structure dislocation forces mark-to-market losses on proprietary books
picks.keyRisk: Stagflationary rate-hike scenario stalls M&A pipeline and equity issuance, the two key IB revenue drivers

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX benefits from both a geopolitical spending supercycle (Iran conflict, NATO 2% GDP commitments, European rearmament) and the commercial aerospace recovery — its Pratt & Whitney engines power next-gen narrow-body aircraft with 24+ month backlogs. Defense names receive the same geopolitical bid as Energy in the current macro regime, with XLI rated among the top 4 sector leaders. RTX's dual commercial/defense revenue split provides natural diversification within the defense subsector, unlike pure-play defense (LMT, NOC) which trade at higher multiples.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (July) with Pratt & Whitney GTF engine delivery ramp and defense contract award flow from supplemental NATO spending packages
picks.catalystWindow: July 2026; ongoing through 2027 defense budget cycle
picks.entryNote: Up ~29.87% total return over the past year per sector data; defense backlogs stretch beyond 24 months giving earnings a sticky floor; trades at a modest discount to LMT/NOC on forward P/E given commercial aviation exposure
picks.exitTrigger: Iran ceasefire leading to rapid defense spending reversal, or Pratt & Whitney GTF engine quality issues triggering material warranty charges beyond current reserves
picks.keyRisk: A sudden Iran peace deal collapsing the geopolitical risk premium embedded in defense multiples

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: Brent crude near $120/barrel with the U.S. naval blockade of Iran extended indefinitely — the Energy sector recorded the largest +8.6% increase in expected dollar-level earnings of all eleven sectors since December 31. XOM's disciplined capex and Permian/LNG integration give it the strongest FCF profile at current oil prices. The sector trades at ~10x forward P/E — deeply discounted vs. the broad market — and XOM's vertically integrated model provides partial downstream hedging. Energy was formally elevated to co-Tier 1 in the May 26 sector update alongside XLK.
picks.catalyst: May/June Brent crude trajectory and any Hormuz Strait supply disruption escalation driving oil to $130+ — plus XOM's Q2 earnings showing FCF conversion at $120 Brent
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~10x forward P/E vs. S&P 500 at 20.9x; energy sector EPS running +46.1% YoY; midstream-integrated model means XOM is less volatile than pure E&P at current oil prices; technically strong trend
picks.exitTrigger: Iran-U.S. ceasefire agreement causing Brent to retreat below $90 on normalized supply restoration, or OPEC+ supply discipline breakdown
picks.keyRisk: Geopolitical resolution (Iran deal) triggers sharp Brent crude selloff, collapsing energy sector earnings estimates rapidly

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: S&P Global is a quality compounder with durable pricing power across ratings, indices, and data/analytics — all of which benefit from elevated capital markets activity and the AI data infrastructure buildout. In a higher-for-longer rate environment, bond issuance activity drives ratings revenue, while Platts commodity data benefits directly from elevated energy price volatility. SPGI has appeared in every single portfolio day since May 15, and the thesis has not been challenged — financial data monopoly with 30%+ operating margins and persistent positive EPS revisions.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (July) showing sustained bond issuance revenue from the AI/data center capex financing wave and continued Commodity Insights growth from energy price volatility
picks.entryNote: SPGI premarket +1.01% on April 28 earnings day; trades at a premium to its historical average (~35x forward) but justified by near-monopoly data moats; positive estimate revision breadth confirmed in momentum screen
picks.exitTrigger: Structural decline in bond issuance volumes, loss of index licensing agreements, or disintermediation of ratings by AI-driven credit scoring platforms
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory pressure on rating agency conflict-of-interest models or forced structural separation of index/ratings businesses

picks.name: Merck & Co. Inc.
picks.rationale: MRK surged 4.5% on May 22 on positive Phase III data for sacituzumab tirumotecan (sac-TMT) plus Keytruda in NSCLC, with the ASCO annual meeting presenting an extensive oncology data wave imminently. Q1 2026 earnings beat expectations and the company raised full-year revenue guidance. Trading at ~$122 against a 52-week low of $75.40 and near its 52-week high of $125.14, MRK has recovered sharply but trades at just ~13x forward earnings — the cheapest mega-cap pharma in the universe and deeply discounted to LLY. The Gardasil China headwind is a known risk being litigated, not an unknown.
picks.catalyst: ASCO Annual Meeting (late May/June 2026) Keytruda pipeline data readouts across multiple cancer indications — the single most important oncology data event of the year
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$122 near 52-week high of $125 — breaking out from a long base; ~13x forward P/E vs. pharma sector average of ~17x; RSI at 55.76 (neutral, not overbought); beta 0.19 gives the portfolio genuine low-correlation defensive quality
picks.exitTrigger: ASCO data disappointment on key Keytruda combinations, Keytruda LOE (2028) guidance pre-announcement without clear pipeline succession, or Gardasil lawsuit adverse ruling
picks.keyRisk: Keytruda patent cliff approaching 2028 without sufficient next-generation pipeline succession established at scale

picks.name: Vistra Energy Corp.
picks.rationale: Vistra is the highest-conviction AI power demand play — a deregulated power generator with nuclear and gas assets positioned directly in the data center electricity demand supercycle. Tepper doubled his VST position in Q1 2026, and the AI data center power narrative has been confirmed by hyperscaler utility contracts. Unlike regulated utilities (NEE, DUK), VST's market-based pricing means it benefits directly from power price appreciation driven by data center load growth, without a 3–7 year regulatory lag. Sector data confirms select power names (VST, CEG) are the only exceptions to the broader utility underweight.
picks.catalyst: Power purchase agreement announcements from major hyperscalers locking in multi-year capacity at premium rates, and Q2 2026 earnings showing nuclear fleet utilization and EBITDA expansion
picks.entryNote: One of the few 'exception' utility names exempt from the rate-sensitive underweight; deregulated model provides direct earnings exposure to power price rather than rate base approval cycle; Tepper doubled position in Q1 2026
picks.exitTrigger: AI data center buildout pauses materially, causing power demand growth expectations to reset lower; or nuclear fleet operational issues raising reliability concerns
picks.keyRisk: AI capex bubble deflation reducing data center power demand forecasts, compressing VST's forward power curve assumptions

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: Gold is up ~9.8% YTD and +129.5% since early 2024, with the Iran war energy shock, potential Fed independence concerns (Warsh era), fiscal deficit at 5.8% of GDP, and the annualized Q2 inflation rate running at 6.85% all providing structural support. In a scenario where the FOMC's next move is a hike rather than a cut, gold historically benefits from the fiscal debasement narrative even as short rates rise. The cleanest zero-counterparty-risk macro hedge against the dominant tail risk: a bond vigilante/sovereign fiscal crisis.
picks.catalyst: May/June CPI print above 4.2% annualized cementing the hiking scenario and driving further safe-haven demand; any Hormuz escalation spike in energy prices
picks.entryNote: Up 9.8% YTD; technically in a strong uptrend with no mean-reversion signals; real rate trajectory is the key variable — if real rates stay near zero or go negative on a hiking-with-inflation path, gold performs well
picks.exitTrigger: Fed achieves credible inflation defeat with real rates rising sharply above 2%, combined with Iran ceasefire reducing geopolitical premium
picks.keyRisk: Hawkish Fed success drives real rates sharply positive, pressuring gold despite fiscal concerns

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD screens for quality dividend-payers with high ROE, FCF yield, and dividend growth track record — the exact factor profile that outperforms in late-cycle regimes when earnings revisions turn negative across cyclicals. The three classic rotation-into-defensives signals (earnings revisions turning, credit spreads potentially widening, hawkish Fed) are all present or emerging as of May 26. Yield ~3.5–4% provides income that partially offsets higher-for-longer rate drag. Low beta vs. Nasdaq provides genuine portfolio volatility reduction.
picks.catalyst: Any credit spread widening event or negative earnings revision wave in cyclicals accelerating defensive rotation into quality income factors
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing; most acute in H2 2026 if hiking scenario materializes
picks.entryNote: Trades at low P/E vs. market (~16-17x) with 3.5-4% yield; historically outperforms SPY in the 6–18 months following yield curve inversion resolution; diversified across financials, healthcare, energy, consumer staples
picks.exitTrigger: Credit spreads remain historically tight and growth re-accelerates, making the income trade-off uncompetitive vs. growth names
picks.keyRisk: Dividend cuts in financials or energy sub-sectors if a rapid macro deterioration hits earnings

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Class B
picks.rationale: With $397B in cash/T-bills vs. a $263B equity book, Berkshire's fortress balance sheet is both a defensive anchor and a call option on a market dislocation — Abel can deploy aggressively at any correction. The $397B cash pile is an explicit macro-caution signal that reinforces the portfolio's defensive tilt at defensiveScore 4. BRK.B trades at ~1.4–1.5x book with zero AI hype premium, providing genuine low-correlation exposure in a drawdown. The restarted buyback ($235M Q1) signals Abel's confidence in intrinsic value at current book multiples.
picks.catalyst: A market correction of 10%+ triggering Berkshire's deployment of cash reserves into high-quality names at distressed valuations — the classic Berkshire optionality event
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing; catalytic in any risk-off event over 1–5 year horizon
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~1.4–1.5x book; buyback program restarted; $397B cash war chest provides asymmetric upside in a market dislocation that would hurt the rest of the book
picks.exitTrigger: Abel materially deploys cash into poor-quality acquisitions at excessive multiples, degrading the capital allocation track record
picks.keyRisk: Prolonged bull market in high-multiple growth means BRK.B significantly underperforms a risk-on tape, creating tracking error vs. SPY

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury ETF
picks.rationale: IEF provides 7–10yr duration as a hedge against the single biggest tail risk in the growth book: a sudden AI capex pause or growth scare that drives flight-to-quality flows into Treasuries. The 3m/10y curve has just re-inverted — a late-cycle signal — and IEF's moderate duration (less long-end fiscal risk than TLT) is the preferred instrument. Bloomberg Intelligence projects 10y finishing 2026 closer to 4.5% than 4.0%, making IEF more defensible than TLT against the persistent fiscal supply pressure at the 30y end.
picks.catalyst: A growth scare (payrolls miss, ISM contraction) or financial stress event driving 10y yields below 4.2% as flight-to-quality dominates the hiking narrative
picks.catalystWindow: H2 2026; most acute if hiring scenario proves overstated and growth data softens
picks.entryNote: 10y at 4.57%; IEF yield approximating ~4.3–4.5% — positive carry while holding a tail-risk hedge; preferred over TLT given CBO fiscal deficit trajectory suppressing long-end demand
picks.exitTrigger: Core PCE falls sustainably below 2.5% and FOMC shifts unambiguously to easing, at which point equity duration risk is preferable to bond duration
picks.keyRisk: Fiscal crisis/bond vigilante scenario drives 10y above 5.5%, causing significant IEF mark-to-market losses while gold and equities also sell off simultaneously

picks.name: Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: KO is Berkshire's 3rd-largest holding at 11.56% of the equity book — one of the clearest quality endorsements available. As a consumer staples compounder with genuine global pricing power, KO provides low-beta income with a ~3% dividend yield and demonstrated ability to pass through inflation via price/mix. In a stagflationary regime where Consumer Discretionary faces fuel surcharge margin pressure, KO's non-discretionary demand profile and geographic diversification provide genuine portfolio ballast. It completes the defensive sleeve's Consumer Staples exposure that SCHD alone cannot fully replicate.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings showing organic revenue growth maintenance above 4% despite energy cost headwinds — validating pricing power thesis in the current inflationary environment
picks.entryNote: Berkshire's 3rd-largest holding at 11.56% of equity book; trades at ~22x forward — premium for staples but justified by global brand moat, pricing power, and ~3% dividend; low beta (0.5–0.6) provides genuine volatility dampening
picks.exitTrigger: Organic revenue growth decelerates below 3% for two consecutive quarters, signaling consumer trade-down away from branded beverages at scale
picks.keyRisk: Consumer staples re-rate lower as capital rotates to cyclicals in a 'soft landing' scenario that removes the defensive premium from the sector

summary: The portfolio is constructed for a late-cycle stagflationary overlay — elevated nominal growth and record earnings coexisting with re-accelerating inflation (Q2 CPI annualizing at ~6.85%), a Fed FOMC majority now leaning toward hikes rather than cuts, and Brent near $120 from the Iran naval blockade. The growth book concentrates in AI infrastructure (NVDA, AVGO, GOOGL, AMZN) which has secular earnings tailwinds that can survive higher rates, flanked by durable quality compounders in Financials (GS, SPGI), defense/industrials (RTX), energy (XOM), and healthcare (LLY, MRK). GOOGL, AVGO, NVDA, AMZN, GS, RTX, XOM, and SPGI are all direct carry-overs from the prior week with theses intact; MRK replaces LLY as the primary Healthcare growth position (LLY moves out given MRK's more asymmetric ASCO catalyst setup and lower valuation), while VST holds its medium-conviction slot replacing META (META's capex overhang at $125–145B guidance warrants a pause pending multiple normalization). The dominant risk being underwritten is an AI capex fatigue event combined with a rate-hike shock that simultaneously compresses high-multiple growth names and triggers credit spread widening — the defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) is specifically sized to absorb that scenario.
diversificationNote: Growth book spans 5 GICS sectors: Information Technology (GOOGL/AVGO/NVDA — 3 names, hard max met, differentiated by value chain: search/cloud, custom ASIC, GPU compute), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN — AWS cloud engine), Financials (GS/SPGI — capital markets vs. data monopoly), Industrials (RTX — defense/aerospace), Energy (XOM), and Health Care (MRK/VST straddles Utilities). Defensive sleeve spans: gold commodity hedge (GLD), dividend quality factor (SCHD), quality-fortress equity with cash optionality (BRK.B), intermediate Treasury duration (IEF), and consumer staples income (KO) — no exposure is doubled.

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: GOOGL delivered a 93.6% Q1 EPS beat at $5.11/share, Google Cloud surged 63% to $20B revenue with a $462B backlog nearly doubling sequentially, and operating margin expanded to 32.9%. Berkshire tripled its stake to ~$16.6B and Third Point initiated a new position in Q1 2026 — making this the single most crowded institutional buy of the quarter. The stock is up ~30% over the past six months, still generating genuine AI monetization, and trades at a more reasonable multiple than peers given the PEG improvement from cloud margin expansion.
picks.catalyst: Google Cloud backlog conversion into revenue and continued AI Overviews/Search monetization in Q2 2026 earnings (late July)
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~27-28x forward EPS — rich vs. 10-year average but justified by cloud margin tripling to 32.9% from 17.8% YoY; consolidating above the 50dma after +30% 6-month run
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud revenue growth decelerates below 40% YoY, or antitrust breakup remedies materially impair Search monetization
picks.keyRisk: DOJ antitrust remedies forcing structural separation of Search and/or Android distribution

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom posted a 9.04% Q1 EPS beat at $2.05 vs $1.88 estimated, with a PEG ratio of just 0.77 despite +106% TTM price return — signaling growth is still underpriced relative to trajectory. ROIC of 21.33% and ROE of 33.37% confirm capital efficiency. Druckenmiller opened a new position in AVGO in Q1 2026, rotating from Nvidia into Broadcom as a more diversified AI infrastructure play across custom silicon (XPUs for Google/Meta/ByteDance) and VMware cloud integration. AI networking spend is a separate, additive driver with 800G optical opportunity seen nearly doubling in 2026.
picks.catalyst: June 3 fiscal Q2 2026 earnings report — custom AI ASIC revenue update and VMware integration synergies
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~31.9x forward P/E vs. historical range of 18-35x; near-term consolidation after +106% TTM run creates a reasonable entry ahead of a high-visibility earnings catalyst
picks.exitTrigger: Loss of custom XPU design wins at Google or Meta, or VMware customer churn above 15%
picks.keyRisk: Customer concentration — top 3 hyperscaler XPU customers represent majority of AI revenue; any single cancellation is a material earnings event

picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA just reported Q1 FY27 revenue of $81.6B (+85% YoY) with $49B free cash flow and 74.9% gross margins, and guided Q2 to $91B vs. $86.84B consensus — a clean upside beat with forward momentum intact. Wedbush called NVDA 'the leading supplier of AI silicon' with a trillion-dollar order book through 2027. The AI infrastructure supercycle nowcast of +11.4% Q2 real gross private domestic investment corroborates sustained hyperscaler capex of $725B budgeted for 2026, up 77%. Position is sized at medium-high rather than maximum given the 'buy the rumor, sell the news' post-earnings dynamic risk and 66% one-year run.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY27 guidance of $91B converts to reported revenue — next print in August 2026, plus Blackwell Ultra ramp confirmation
picks.entryNote: Trading around $135-145 range post-May 20 print; stock has historically seen post-earnings chop before resuming trend — current entry is into the post-report consolidation window
picks.exitTrigger: Gross margin compression below 70% or Q3 guidance below $90B, signaling Blackwell supply constraints exceeding demand
picks.keyRisk: Post-earnings 'sell the news' sentiment reset; hyperscaler debt-financed capex could be trimmed if rates stay elevated and FCF pressure mounts

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is a high-conviction defense compounder with continuous contract flow — a $441.6M PATRIOT GEM-T modification for Operation Epic Fury, a $335M Standard Missile modification, SeaRAM frigate contracts with Australia, and Space Force interceptor program selection all announced in May 2026. The DoD's aggressive shift toward autonomous AI-driven tactical networks and hypersonic programs directly benefits RTX's Raytheon segment. The 12-month analyst consensus target of $215.27 implies 22.3% upside from current levels, with a 23-analyst Buy consensus and a 7.4% dividend increase just declared. Middle East conflict spending mandates are a structural, multi-year tailwind.
picks.catalyst: CEO Chris Calio speaking at Bernstein Strategic Decisions Conference May 29 — expected to provide backlog and margin guidance update
picks.catalystWindow: May 29, 2026 (next 7 days) then Q2 earnings July
picks.entryNote: Trading roughly 15-17% below consensus analyst price target; forward P/E ~20x on defense earnings that are recession-resistant; consolidating above long-term support after steady re-rating higher
picks.exitTrigger: Middle East ceasefire reducing defense procurement urgency, or GTF engine liability charges materially exceeding $7B reserve
picks.keyRisk: Pratt & Whitney GTF powder metal engine liability remains an open-ended balance sheet risk that could resurface with incremental charges

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Financials are the #2 ranked sector in the rotation screen, with Goldman specifically positioned as the premier capital markets beneficiary of deal flow reactivation and a steeper yield curve. Outside of IT and Energy, Financials is the only sector with positive dollar-level earnings revisions since December 31. Goldman's trading and advisory desks benefit directly from volatility and geopolitical uncertainty — regimes that are suppressive for most sectors are additive to Goldman's revenue mix. The sector trades at a discount to the broader market with improving EPS and dividend trajectory, and institutional flows are reaccelerating with SCHD's 2026 reconstitution raising Financials from 9.7% to 15%+.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings report (mid-July) — trading revenue and M&A advisory pipeline against backdrop of reactivating deal markets
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~10-11x forward earnings — meaningful discount to SPX at ~20x; yield curve steepening (2s10s turning positive) directly expands NII for banking conglomerates; technically in an uptrend
picks.exitTrigger: Credit cycle turns with HY OAS widening above 450bps, or capital markets deal flow stalls in a recessionary environment
picks.keyRisk: Late-cycle credit deterioration — Goldman's trading book has tail risk to a rapid HY spread blow-out if recession materializes faster than consensus expects

picks.name: Eli Lilly and Company
picks.rationale: LLY is trading at ~$1,044 today (May 22) after its next-generation retatrutide weight-loss drug cleared a late-stage Phase 3 clinical trial — a direct pipeline catalyst adding to the already-dominant GLP-1 oligopoly with Mounjaro and Zepbound. The company has committed ~$23B in manufacturing capex to expand fill-finish capacity through 2027, removing the supply ceiling that capped 2025 revenue. Unlike NVO, which faces semaglutide patent cliffs and MFN pricing pressure, LLY benefits from NVO's headwinds by capturing market share. Druckenmiller increased his LLY stake to 100,675 shares in Q1 2026, validating the quality compounder thesis.
picks.catalyst: Retatrutide Phase 3 full data readout and potential FDA filing timeline — plus Q2 earnings in late July with Zepbound volume trajectory
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$1,044, well below 52-week high of $1,133; ~30-35x forward earnings is reasonable for a 20%+ earnings growth compounder with secular obesity + Alzheimer's pipeline; bouncing off multi-month support
picks.exitTrigger: MFN pricing executive order expands to commercial markets, or oral GLP-1 competitor (e.g., Pfizer danuglipron) achieves superior efficacy data in Phase 3
picks.keyRisk: Medicare/Medicaid GLP-1 coverage rollback under 'One Big Beautiful Bill' reduces total addressable market and realized pricing

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is a capital-light, high-ROIC compounder with structural revenue tied to debt issuance, index licensing, and data analytics — all tailwinds in a late-cycle environment where corporate debt issuance remains robust and passive index AUM continues compounding. HY OAS at 276bps (near multi-year tights) and IG OAS at ~80bps reflect a functioning credit market still generating ratings and issuance fees. SPGI's Market Intelligence and Commodity Insights segments diversify revenue beyond pure credit cycles. The business has pricing power embedded in long-term contracts and a near-monopoly in index licensing alongside MSCI.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — debt issuance volumes in Q2 driven by corporate refinancing activity and sovereign bond supply will directly confirm revenue trajectory
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~28-30x forward earnings — below its 5-year average premium of 33x; credit market stability at current spread levels supports a stable issuance fee baseline going into Q2
picks.exitTrigger: HY OAS widens sharply above 400bps (credit stress kills issuance), or regulatory challenge to ratings oligopoly business model
picks.keyRisk: A sharp credit cycle turn would reduce debt issuance and ratings fees simultaneously — SPGI's earnings are more cyclical than its quality multiple implies

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: XOM is a portfolio anchor against the Middle East conflict / energy price tail risk — gas prices are up 28.4% YoY averaging $4.50/gallon, and Energy sector Q2 estimate revisions have been positive since the start of the Iran war. XOM trades at ~11x forward P/E with a 3.4% dividend yield and best-in-class integration across upstream, refining, and chemicals, making it the most resilient major if oil prices spike on conflict escalation. The Pioneer acquisition has added 1.3B BOE of Permian inventory at low breakevens (~$35/bbl), providing durable FCF even in a $70 crude scenario.
picks.catalyst: April Core PCE print (May 30) — a hot print above 3.1% removes rate cut expectations entirely, supporting energy sector multiple; any Middle East escalation is an immediate price catalyst
picks.catalystWindow: May 30 PCE print and rolling geopolitical news flow
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~11x forward P/E vs. sector median of 12-13x and 5-year XOM average of 14x; Chevron has been trimmed by both Berkshire and institutional investors — XOM is the higher-quality alternative at a similar valuation
picks.exitTrigger: WTI crude sustains below $65/bbl for 60+ days, implying demand destruction that compresses XOM FCF below $30B annualized
picks.keyRisk: Global growth deceleration (particularly China demand) brings crude below $65 — XOM's Permian breakeven is low but the multiple would still compress

picks.name: Vistra Energy Corp.
picks.rationale: VST is the purest public market play on AI data center power demand growth — owning a mix of nuclear, gas, and retail electricity that directly benefits from the +11.4% Q2 real gross private domestic investment nowcast driven by AI capex. Tepper doubled his VST position in Q1 2026, and the DoE has flagged US power grid capacity as the binding constraint on AI infrastructure scaling. Unlike regulated utilities, VST has merchant price upside when power demand surges. Here is your full macro briefing as of Friday, May 22, 2026.

---

## 📊 US EQUITY MACRO REGIME BRIEFING — May 22, 2026

---

### DATA DASHBOARD

**FED POLICY**

The FOMC held the target range for the fed funds rate at **3.50%–3.75%** at its March 18, 2026 meeting.
 
The March 2026 dot plot median signals only **25 bps of cuts in 2026 and 25 bps in 2027**, with the Fed projecting PCE inflation to end 2026 at **2.7%** — 30 bps higher than its December 2025 projection.
 However, 
markets now broadly expect no cuts this year, with one analyst noting: "The trajectory of inflation will not immediately reverse, even if geopolitical tensions ease, making it highly unlikely that we will see any interest rate cuts this year."
 The Fed's longer-run neutral rate is estimated around 3.0%, implying the real policy rate (~+0.8% above 2.7% projected inflation) remains only modestly restrictive. 
Importantly, Kevin Warsh assumed the Fed Chair role on May 15, replacing Jerome Powell.


**YIELD CURVE**

As of May 21, the US yield curve shape is *Normal* — both 2s10s and 3m10y are nominally positive — with the current policy rate at **4.25%** (upper bound).
 
The 10-year yield sits roughly around **4.20%**, while the 2s10s spread is positive but narrow, and the 3m10y has only just crossed below zero.
 
That combination — front-end inverted, belly flat, long end slightly higher — is a *classic late-cycle signature*. The 3m10y spread has flipped negative again in recent weeks after a brief positive excursion in early 2026.
 The 30-year was at **~4.88%** as of mid-March. Term premium is directionally rising — driven by fiscal supply, energy-shock uncertainty, and the Fed leadership transition. 
The 2s10s, which inverted continuously between mid-2022 and late-2024, is now narrowly positive but well below the 100–150 bp range typical of a healthy expansion.


**INFLATION**

April CPI: Core CPI rose **+0.4% m/m**, with inflation ticking up 0.2 ppt to **2.8% y/y**.
 
Shelter costs rose 0.6% m/m, though a temporary statistical distortion tied to last fall's government shutdown lifted shelter CPI in April — a one-off effect unlikely to signal underlying momentum.
 
The bifurcation between goods and services is the new feature of April 2026: goods are flat or declining while services accelerate — consistent with a tariff regime absorbed in margins, layered onto a services-side cost shock being passed through directly.
 
Core PCE stood at **3.0% YoY** through February
; 
April core PCE (due May 30) will likely run cooler than +0.4% core CPI, with consensus expecting roughly **0.25–0.35% m/m**, pushing YoY toward **2.9–3.1%**.
 
Supercore (services ex-shelter) showed no meaningful deterioration in March
, but 
services prices in April revealed early passthrough from the Middle East conflict, with airfare rising strongly.
 Inflation is **re-accelerating** at the headline level (energy/services shock), sticky at core, with a tail risk of entrenchment.

**GROWTH**

The Atlanta Fed GDPNow model estimate for real GDP growth (SAAR) in Q2 2026 is **+4.3%** as of May 21, up from 4.0% on May 14.
 
Nowcasts of Q2 real PCE growth and real gross private domestic investment growth increased to **2.9%** and **11.4%**, respectively.
 
The BEA's first estimate of Q1 2026 real GDP growth was **+2.0%**, 0.8 ppt above the final GDPNow nowcast.
 The payrolls run-rate remains solid (~175–185K/month average over recent months), with unemployment projected by the Fed at 4.4% in Q4 2026. ISM Manufacturing remains in contraction territory near 48–49; ISM Services is modestly expansionary near 51–52.

**CREDIT & LIQUIDITY**

ICE BofA US HY OAS was **~2.76%** (276 bps) in May 2026
 — near multi-year tights and not signaling stress. 
Broad investment-grade OAS is roughly **~80 bps** as of early 2026, with tighter spreads for AA issuers and wider for BBB.
 Financial conditions remain broadly accommodative. 
The DXY (USD index) closed at approximately **99.31** on May 22
, reflecting mild dollar softness — a tailwind for US multinationals and EM.

**EARNINGS**

The S&P 500 forward four-quarter EPS estimate (FFQE) closed last week at approximately **$346.82**, with the earnings yield at **4.68%** as of May 15.
 
Excluding non-operating gains from GOOG, AMZN, and NFLX, Q1 y-o-y S&P 500 EPS growth falls to approximately **10%**, with 2026 annual growth likely around 22%.
 
The trailing S&P 500 earnings yield is **3.71%** as of May 22
, implying a trailing P/E near **~27x** — stretched against a 10-year average closer to 18–19x forward. 
As of March 31, equities were assessed as *Fairly Valued* on an earnings yield gap basis, 0.30 standard deviations below historical average.


---

### ¶1 — REGIME CALL

**This is a late-cycle regime with a geopolitical overlay.** 
The yield curve exhibits a classic late-cycle signature: front-end effectively inverted on 3m10y, the 2s10s narrowly positive after continuous inversion from 2022–2024, and the 3m10y flipping back negative in recent weeks.
 The policy rate at 3.50–3.75% remains above neutral (~3.0%), labor markets are still tight but moderating (Fed projects 4.4% unemployment by year-end), 
goods prices are flat or declining while services are accelerating
 — a textbook late-expansion price dynamic. 
The dot plot pencils in only 50 bps of total cuts through 2027, with PCE stuck at 2.7% this year
, and 
market pricing has effectively removed rate cuts from the 2026 calendar entirely.
 The combination of positive but decelerating real growth, sticky-to-rising inflation, a frozen Fed, and a leadership transition at the FOMC is a *late-cycle / pre-contraction* positioning environment — not yet a contraction, but with material fat tails.

---

### ¶2 — WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING

In a late-cycle, above-neutral-rate, sticky-inflation regime, the playbook favors **quality, cash flow durability, and real asset exposure over duration and pure growth**. What's **working**: (1) **Large-cap Energy and Defense** — 
gas prices are up ~28.4% YoY and averaging $4.50/gallon
, directly supporting Energy sector earnings and defense spending mandates linked to the Middle East conflict; (2) **AI Infrastructure / Hyperscalers** — 
Q2 real gross private domestic investment is nowcasting at +11.4%
, with AI capex the primary driver, and 
EPS growth ex-non-operating items is still ~10% y/y
; (3) **Short-duration IG Credit** — 
IG OAS at ~80 bps
 is tight but credit is not breaking, and carry is attractive at current all-in yields; (4) **Consumer Staples / Pricing Power** as a defensive hedge. What's **breaking**: (1) **Rate-sensitive / long-duration equities** (REITs, utilities, speculative growth) — 
a 10-year yield above 5% would be meaningfully negative, especially amid Fed leadership transition and curve steepening
; (2) **Consumer Discretionary** — 
food prices up 3.2% YoY and beef up 14.8%
 are squeezing real purchasing power; (3) **HY issuers with floating-rate debt** — spreads are tight, but refinancing at 3.50%+ policy rates is compressing margins in leveraged capital structures.

---

### ¶3 — WHAT TO WATCH (2–3 Highest-Impact Events, Next 1–6 Months)

1. **April Core PCE (May 30)** — 
The Fed's preferred gauge; PCE weights shelter less heavily than CPI, so April core PCE will likely run cooler than +0.4% core CPI, but consensus expects YoY ticking toward **2.9–3.1%** from March's 3.0%.
 A print above 3.1% would effectively end any remaining H2 2026 cut probability and reprice the long end materially higher.

2. **Iran/Middle East Conflict Resolution — Energy Price Trajectory** — 
Economists note inflationary effects of the war could take weeks to months to unwind, with an optimistic normalization timeline of ~2 months if resolved soon, or six to nine months in a pessimistic scenario.
 
Lingering geopolitical tensions in the Middle East are likely to add further upward pressure through higher fuel costs, feeding into transportation prices in the coming months.
 An escalation or a durable ceasefire is the single highest-variance input to the inflation trajectory and Fed optionality.

3. **New Fed Chair Warsh's First Communications / FOMC Policy Signals** — 
Warsh assumed the Chair role on May 15; his first communications will be parsed carefully for whether he frames April's inflation data as an energy outlier (dovish read) or a services breakout (hawkish read).
 Any pivot in the Fed's reaction function under new leadership — particularly on the inflation tolerance threshold — is the key institutional risk for the rate/equity complex over the next 1–6 months. Watch the June FOMC (June 17–18) and the updated SEP dot plot as the definitive signal.

---
*All data as of market close May 22, 2026. Forward-looking statements are analytical, not investment advice.*

## SECTION 3 — MACRO TAIL RISKS (12 MONTHS)
### ⚠️ Three Highest-Impact Regime-Breaking Risks

---

### TAIL RISK #1: U.S. Fiscal / Sovereign Bond Crisis
**Probability Tier: ELEVATED**


The CBO projects the federal budget deficit at $1.9 trillion in fiscal 2026, growing to $3.1 trillion by 2036. Relative to GDP, the deficit is 5.8% in 2026, far above the 3.8% historical average. Rising net interest costs drive much of that increase.



The general government deficit is expected to remain in the 7–7.5% of GDP range over the medium term, with debt exceeding 140% of GDP by 2031.



Investors have been surprisingly immune to concerns about the trajectory of the U.S. fiscal deficit. But America's public finances are precarious; the deficit is expected to remain at 6–7% for some time. There's a risk investors begin to balk at the volume of debt issuance — potentially if fiscal profligacy is combined with a perception of too-loose monetary policy and hints of inflation, causing bond yields to spike.


**Trigger:** A failed Treasury auction, Moody's/Fitch downgrade action, or a debt ceiling standoff in early 2027 (CBO projects Treasury hits the limit in 2027). 
If the Fed fails to tame inflation, or its independence is viewed as compromised, then long-term rates could spike.


**Factors/Sectors Hurt Most:**
- 📉 **TLT, IEF** (long-duration bonds crushed)
- 📉 **Utilities (NEE, DUK, SO)** — heavily rate-sensitive, re-rate down
- 📉 **REITs (PLD, EQIX, AMT, WELL)** — cap rates spike, NAVs compress
- 📉 **High-multiple growth (PLTR, SNOW, ARM)** — discount rates surge, DCFs evaporate
- 📈 **GLD** — the primary beneficiary in this scenario
